root_text.sn13.4:1.2: “Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, yatthimā mahānadiyo saṁsandanti samenti, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn13.4:1.3: gaṅgā yamunā aciravatī sarabhū mahī, taṁ udakaṁ parikkhayaṁ pariyādānaṁ gaccheyya ṭhapetvā dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni.
root_text.sn13.4:1.5: katamaṁ nu kho bahutaraṁ, yaṁ vā sambhejjaudakaṁ parikkhīṇaṁ pariyādiṇṇaṁ yāni vā dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni avasiṭṭhānī”ti?
root_text.sn13.4:2.1: “Etadeva, bhante, bahutaraṁ sambhejjaudakaṁ yadidaṁ parikkhīṇaṁ pariyādiṇṇaṁ;
root_text.sn13.4:2.2: appamattakāni dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni avasiṭṭhāni.
root_text.sn13.4:2.3: Neva satimaṁ kalaṁ upenti na sahassimaṁ kalaṁ upenti na satasahassimaṁ kalaṁ upenti sambhejjaudakaṁ parikkhīṇaṁ pariyādiṇṇaṁ upanidhāya dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni avasiṭṭhānī”ti.

translation_text.sn13.4:1.2: “Mendicants, there are places where the great rivers—the Ganges, Yamuna, Aciravatī, Sarabhū, and Mahī—join together and converge.
translation_text.sn13.4:1.3: Suppose that water dried up and evaporated except for two or three drops.
translation_text.sn13.4:1.5: Which is more: the water in the confluence that has dried up and evaporated, or the two or three drops left?”
translation_text.sn13.4:2.1: “Sir, the water in the confluence that has dried up and evaporated is certainly more.
translation_text.sn13.4:2.2: The two or three drops left are tiny.
translation_text.sn13.4:2.3: Compared to the water in the confluence that has dried up and evaporated, it’s not nearly a hundredth, a thousandth, or a hundred thousandth part.”
translation_text.sn13.4:2.4: “In the same way, for a noble disciple, the suffering that’s over and done with is more …”
